sentences.text: The survivor is Mrs.
Bella Sobol.
This is tape three.
So, you know, when we was making the roads, and we saw from what we make at the roads, we were standing and crying.
I said, maybe it's our families' bones.
It was unbearable, unbearable thing.
And wherever we was turning, we couldn't even cry.
We couldn't do nothing, because constantly around us was the Germans, you know, the SS with the German Shepherds.
I would like-- see, I forgot something.
I would like to come back about my sister.
In the beginning when I came to the camp, the four weeks afterwards, it happened to my sister.
She got frozen toe.
Hanka.
Hanka.
This was the only sister who came back, who I found her.
And she said she cannot go to work.
I said, what means you cannot go to work?
You know what's going to happen to you?
She said, I cannot put on the shoe.
I cannot.
I have to stay here.
So I knew when I'm going to come back, Hanka is no more going to be here.
So I said goodbye to her, I kissed her, and I went to work and Hanka stayed in the barracks, in the block.
She didn't stay in the blocks.
All of them who ever stayed, who was left there, they put them together.
They brought them to the block what I described before, where the dead and the half dead was.
All of them had to go to there until the trucks came there.
The trucks supposed to come there to pick them up, take them to crematorium because they couldn't-- these kind of people couldn't walk to crematorium.
They was all dead or half dead.
And I went to work, and I said to myself-- I hold me my other friends, I said, you're going to come back and Hanka's not going to be no more there.
So I couldn't wait already to be already in block to see what's going-- I mean, to be back in the camp.
So I came back to the camp.
That was just a few minutes before the counting, so they let us go in the barracks.
When I went in the barracks I ran the other way.
Where I'm going?
To my place where I had my bed there.
Hanka's sitting.
And I said, Hanka, what are you doing here?
I said, God sent you back.
So she told me the story.
A transport, you know, a new train came with people and they needed the trucks.
And they opened up the gates, they said everybody goes back.
I said, Hanka, tomorrow you're not going to stay here.
You're going.
Whatever is going to happen to you, you're not staying here.
The thing was, it was winter.
It was, I think, in February.
This was February probably, and it was very-- you know, the ground, it was-- I said, we're going to do something.
So I said, how about I'm going to do with my toe?
So there's no water, don't forget.
There was no water, no latrines.
I even forgot to tell you the circumstances, in the beginning what was going on in Birkenau.
There was nothing.
There was a latrine.
All there was, before the barracks there was staying a [? barrel. ?] And at night who could go down went to the [? barrel. ?]
Note, they did in the bowl and they just emptied it out on somebody else.
So I went-- I said, maybe I can do something.
I got-- you know, at night we got the piece of bread.
I said I'm going to-- I think I had a little idea of what to do.
So there was a block, there was a group of people who worked the Kanada.
And these people always brought back some things.
They're organizing.
They were filling-- What was Kanada?
There was-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the Kanada was the things what they brought back from the dead people, all the clothes.
So I knew-- the people was telling me they are organizing things, and they bring them.
And you have a piece of bread or a piece of margarine-- whatever you have, you can buy some stuff there.
It was like a black market.
So I went in there and I met a girl.
She was I don't know from where.
Not far from my home town.
And coincidentally, she had a soft shoe.
This I would never believe in my life.
I'm going to bring something to Hanka.
I gave exchange with her.
I gave her the shoe-- I gave her the bread, and I brought in the shoe.
And we took the urine and we took that piece of blanket.
We tore a piece of blanket, what we covered ourselves, and we washed down the toe and the right leg foot.
And next day we put on the shoes, covered it up-- I don't know if we had a stockings or not-- and Hanka went to work.
Hanka went to work.
It was OK.
You know, she could work.
There was nothing wrong with Hanka, just the toe was frozen, and this means death.
That meant death.
So, yeah, a few days later the girl brought another shoe.
I gave her, again, the bread, and Hanka had two shoes.
And, you know, until everything started thawing outside, Hanka could put on her shoes, what she had, or, you know, the wooden shoes or the regular shoes.
Hanka was saved.
Hanka was safe.
Hanka went to work, you know.
And, you know, the spring came.
Was a little better, like I describe before.
And between-- the work was constantly working outside, until we got a break to a friend who knew our father.
And he knew [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] the superintendent on the barrack.
And she took my number-- this was the name, the number-- and she told me-- they told us-- they told me to come to the other place.
Next day I'm going to go to the Kanada to work.
You cannot imagine what it meant to go to the Kanada to work.
I didn't know what's going on.
Kanada consisted in a lot of barracks.
Nobody lived there.
There was just a place where we came in.
And constantly, day and night, trucks came in with the things, with the clothing, from the dead.
Not from the people who was wearing them in the concentration-- from the dead.
All the clothing, all the suitcases, all the pots and pans, whatever you think in the world.
They brought everything, and we had to sort this.
So every barrack had another mission.
One barrack was sorting [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] linen.
One barrack was sorting shoes.
One barrack was sorting pots and pans.
So we were staying by piles high to the sky.
You had to sort it.
Whatever was dirty, or what we didn't like or that they didn't like, they told us take a knife and cut it, and put in the side.
And I put-- you know, and I was sorting from the piles, let me say, clothes and shoes here and dresses here.
And the other girls, they stayed by the tables and you had to make bundles.
Underwear bundles-- men's underwear, women's underwear, bras, girdles.
Whatever you touched, everything had a name, and it had to be just right.
And this was going on, you know, day in and day out.
And that was good because between the bundles we found some food in the packets from the jackets.
We found-- there was plenty money there.
There was whatever you-- you just could imagine.
We could-- we had to check everything.
When we was looking through the clothes, we had to check up under the buttons, and the cushions, and the seams, and the seams from the pants and the seams from the skirts.
Whatever, you know, we saw something that was inside, we had a razor blade in hand.
We had to open up.
And there was staying a big box-- boxes.
Big, big boxes.
We had to through everything in it in.
This was German.
There was a German supervisor.
She was to run the concentration.
And the Germans.
They had an office there.
They had everything.
So when the people was making the bundles, these bundles went to special barracks, and they was going up till the sky.
And afterwards, the trains-- there was right away a train station by.
The trains came, they was loading on the trains.
All these clothes went to Germany.
Clothes, whatever was there.
Whatever the Jews brought, they took it.
So one day I-- yeah.
I was staying and sorting the clothes, I came across a girdle.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In Europe the girdles, there was iron inside.
Years ago people was wearing them.
And I start-- you know.
I said, this is something heavy.
So there was [INAUDIBLE] and there was a plate.
You know, the German officer-- so I took the girdle, and I pushed it to the side, and I knew where I put it away.
I said, when he's going to go out, I'm going to take the razor blade and I'm going to slice it.
It was the whole girdle was with dollars, hundred dollars.
Hundred dollars.
So much money.
I didn't even know what dollars-- I understood.
And there was some other money.
There was white papers.
I didn't know what this is.
So later they told me it was English pounds.
And this I tore in little pieces, and I threw it away in the garbage.
And the dollars I put my pocket.
I said, I'm going to try to do something with them.
So before we went home I put them in my shoes.
So I never wore big shoes.
When they saw somebody was wearing big shoes, they told them to take off the shoes and they looked in.
So people put in some silk thinks, you know, some other things so they brought home to, you know, to sell it.
So I brought home the money.
I went through.
I was lucky, I went through.
They didn't tell me to-- you know, I put my life in my hands.
Right away, they would kill me.
So I brought home.
You know, the other girls who was living with us together, they had husbands.
So there was a few thousand bucks.
I said, what are we going to do with the money here?
Where can you hide the money?
So we took the money, you know, and we gave to the men, and the men bought bread.
For our bread, $100.
Because Polish people came in-- [INAUDIBLE] People came in to work in the camps.
They was working for the crematoriums.
They was building other barracks.
They needed electricians.
There was, between our people, plenty work.
So there were outside people.
Outside, yeah.
Sometimes they brought in outside people.
So anyway, so we, you know-- I said, listen, we have money now.
So the money lasted maybe a total of two weeks, because you have hundred of hundreds of dollars for bread.
So I work there for a few months, and, you know, Hanka was already have another-- my sister had already another place to work.
She didn't have to go out, outside working.
And, you know-- and there was-- you know, we was one of the lucky ones.
We had something to eat.
It was, you know, very good, until one day, you know, my advisor said to take some stuff for her.
And I put-- I always wore a girdle, so I could make my girdle-- everybody was slim, so in the girdle we put in all that stuff.
So I took for her and I took for myself.
So this day, the German, the [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], shout on me and said, take off your clothes.
I had to take off everything.
Everything fall out.
So he said-- he took my number.
This was the name, and I knew now he's going to send me to the crematorium or he's going to kill me.
So after a few days, coming back, you know, I told the gills what happened to me, this and that.
And we was waiting for the verdict.
What's going to be the verdict.
So they called me to the front.
In the barracks they had an office.
They called me in there, and my heart was beating like a horse.
And I said to myself, what's going to happen to you, Bella, now?
So Bella-- so he said to go back to the barrack.
They're going to shave my head.
I had already a little hair.
Was already, you know-- I was already this-- probably seven, eight months, and I had already good clothes wearing because I was working-- every day I put on some other things.
I sold them, and I put on old ones, and I left the old ones, and I put on new ones.
So he said, you are no more going to this kommando.
You're going back there.
They're going to cut your hair.
They're going to do just like in the beginning they did to me.
And I'm going to go work outside kommando.
Outside kommando there was, again, to the bed.
So I said, thank God they didn't kill me.
So I went to other, you know, all kind of work.
I had to work.
Oh, I had you know-- I knew already people, so after a few weeks of working outside I got myself into another kommando in the camp, in another kommando.
This was already the end of 1943.
So my other sisters-- my sister, my other friends-- all of them had already places where to work.
And this was going until 1940-- so, I mean, in the beginning of 1944 they start building-- putting together a new kommando.
A kommando-- we didn't know what.
They said to everyone stay closer, we was between.
I said, we're going to go.
They picked us up, hundreds, a few hundred girls, and we went to [INAUDIBLE] [INAUDIBLE], there was outside the perimeter of Birkenau, because Birkenau was already hundreds of barracks big.
When we came, there was two, and by 1944 they start bringing people from all over Europe, the whole time.
And this time they had to bring the Hungarian Jews.
There was a hundred thousand, two hundred thousand.
So they built a new blocks, and there was a big place where to bring them in, the people.
And the crematoriums was walking distance where I stayed.
I saw it.
So we went there.
And the bell began.
The people start coming.
Until now I never saw people walking.
I never saw people going to crematorium.
This time I'm really a witness, I really saw.
And this I will never forget, how the mothers and the children with the parched lips, with the burning faces-- and in summer how they was walking in from both sides.
The Germans were standing and watching them with the dogs.
And for me-- and I was staying outside by the fence.
And I tried not to look at them because I was afraid the Germans were going to shoot me.

I was living there, and I saw the mothers, and I said, my gosh, maybe that's my mother, my father-- happened the same-- they're going in to this place 100 feet from me, beautiful trees, green, everything, and in this place there was staying the crematorium.
There was four crematoriums.
And yet, these people don't know where they're going.
They still don't know.
Nobody told them nothing.
It's already five years, the war.
Nobody knew nothing.
So they went there.
You never heard a noise, you never heard a scream.
They took them in.
And some of them, they were saying still outside.
They couldn't take in all of the people who came in.
They took them, so many in the room.
They undressed them.
They told them the same thing, to put nice together the clothes.
And they opened up another room, and there was sprinklers, and they went inside.
And whatever they did, this I know.
The outside the crematorium, there was little windows what I saw, and through the little windows they put in the Zyklon.
Little boxes, blue boxes.
The name I never forget, because I could read it from far away.
And they put in the people, and they came out the other side.
The other side there was already-- there was a whole group of people working, the sonderkommando.
They was dragging them out, all they took them to the crematoriums.
But there was so many coming the crematorium couldn't burn them so many.
So they had holes.
They make big holes with wood.
They was throwing them in the holes.
And the other people were staying outside didn't see nothing, just like nothing happened to them.
And these people again.
And this was going on day in and day out.
And working, and working, and working, and burning.
The smoke, the smell, for months, for years I could feel-- even till today when somebody's burning bones, chickens, meat, you have in your nostrils.
So this was going on a whole summer from April, I think, until September, October.
And they brought from all over the Jews.
Wherever there was a Jew they brought in the Jews.
From Lodz, from-- people who was hiding-- even people who-- there was-- people who was hiding in any place, they found them.
What year was this?
1944-- this was already by the end of 1944.
And there was no more transports.
One day there was no more transports.
They said there is no more.
They're not going to bring no more.
There was no more.
So there was-- they had plenty of things to sort there.
You can imagine how many things we have.
This place where-- this [INAUDIBLE] it was special built.
All the barracks, everything was filled with the goods from the people.
Constantly, the trains-- the trucks came, loaded, day in and day out, to take away the stuff.
And I was working when the people-- the people who was selected to come to the camp, I was working on the side in the bath.
There was a dirty side and a clean side.
I was working where the people had to undress.
On the other side there was working people who would dress them.
So their clothes, they have to leave everything.
The same thing like I came.
So the people, they didn't know from nothing.
The people who came from Poland who worked already in working camps, they had a little idea.
They had a little idea.
Or the people who came from Hungary, or they came from wherever.
I don't know from where they brought.
I know from Hungary was the most in 1944.
They didn't have the faintest idea, even the people who came in.
So we undressed them.
And from our place they had to go in where they cleaned them.
They cleaned them, they shaved them, they cleaned them.
So one time, a mother came in with her child.
Was a Polish Jew.
The girl was maybe eight years old.
I don't know how she went through with the mother-- the mother went through, or how she came through.
She was-- I think they was from a working camp.
She came in, she said to us, we don't know what to do.
The Germans are going to come in, they're going to take away the child.
So we said, don't worry.
We took the child.
There was a bathroom.
The bathroom-- so we took the child first-- No, first we took the child, and the people was taking off the clothes.
We make a big pile and we put the child in, because the Germans not always was staying near us.
We put the child in, and when the mother went on the other side to get cleaned, and nobody was looking to us, we took the child out.
And, fast, we said to her.
Run.
And the mother was staying, watching.
And the mother got reunited with the child, and they went through.
So the mother told me-- I don't know what was her name.
I know she said she has a husband in Argentina, and they came, I think, from a place-- Plaszow something, from a working camp.
And the child-- we were so happy we saved the child.
And so this was going on until, you know, everything finished up.
There was no more transports.
There was no more transports, so we said, what's going to happen to us?
So they said they have a new place.
And I was-- we was always from the first.
We said, we're going to go.
We didn't know where we're going to go.
So took that group of people, maybe 50 girls, and they took us back between Birkenau-Auschwitz.
They built a model place, a model, that was consisted of four barracks.
There was beds, there was linen, there was a shower, there was a basement, recreation-- there was everything in it.
Now I understand this was built for the Red Cross.
In case they come in, they had to show them how the people-- the prisoners live.
Did it have a name?
No-- had not a name.
Maybe it had a name, I don't know.
I didn't think about it.
This was between Auschwitz and Birkenau.
So we stayed there.
We came there in October, and we went back to work to the old Kanada, to the old place.
The place was empty, but there were still some you know, old pieces, the old clothes.
So they told us to sort this, to tear it.
There was no work, no more work.
And this place, you know, the place where I stayed now, consisted of a few kommandos.
There was a kommando who worked about ammunition.
This is what I want to bring in now, the ammunition.
And we worked, and there was even a hospital there.
You can imagine, somebody was sick, went in, you know, he had a chance to come out.
So between-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] maybe later we have to put this in the end, or something together.
In this camp, in this place, I want to tell a story how they-- there was, you know, some girls.
This happened-- this still-- I have to go back now.
This, what I want to tell now, happened in [INAUDIBLE].
There was an organization, an underground organization.
I forgot about to tell this.
And we didn't know nothing about this.
We just knew that something's going on, because we was already old prisoners.
We had, you know, like they're saying, you know, news.
It was traveling, the news.
And they came to us, they said when we're going to find money, when we're going to look through the clothes, not to give to the Germans.
To hide it in places.
And they told us where to bring it.
So there was pots, and kettles, and things.
So we always-- there was a special place where all these pots and the pans had a barrack.
They said to throw the money in, and to take a big bundle, and to bring it there.
There, somebody was there who took the money from us.
And they told us, this money goes for the underground organization, and they're buying bullets, ammunition, whatever they can.
We was happy with them.
Before they took the money-- when I found the money already in [INAUDIBLE], I went in the toilet.
I had full pockets.
And I pulled the chain, and I never put in the money to them.
I put everything, all the money, you know?
So now I knew something, so I took the money.
And so we heard something is going to happen.
And one day it really happened.
This was, I think, October.
I think October, she says, in the papers.
We heard a boom, shooting, and they blew up the crematoriums.
Near my barrack was one crematorium.
They don't use that.
The people, the sonderkommando, lived in this block.
So I run out and there was a bench.
I stood up on the bench, and there was-- between me and them, there was a big fence.
I stood up on the fence and I heard shooting, and the block, the building, was on fire.
And ten minutes later, from the other side-- there was probably two kilometers-- we heard silence.
And they blew it up, the other crematorium.
So there I didn't saw nothing.
Here I saw.
The Germans came, the SS men came, and they started shooting.
They start shooting, and all the people who was in the block.
All the sonderkommando-- there was a night shift, there was a day shift, there was a middle day, there was three shifts going-- they killed them all.
They killed them all.
So afterwards when everything was quiet-- you know, many, many people got killed-- they start making inquiry for where the Jews, or the organization-- the underground had.
And they start looking until they came across a girl named Roza Robota.
This Roza Robota was in contact with girls who worked in the ammunition.
This-- Go ahead.
These girls got-- she, Roza, brought-- she gave them bread, she gave them other things.
And they were stealing, you know, bullets.
When they start making inquiries they came to her.
She was the first in all the other girls.
The other girls, I don't know.
I don't know they was talking or not.
I know one thing.
Roza never-- they took her and it's called the political-- a special political prison.
And they did to her terrible things.
She didn't want to say another word.
